metadata.seed: Well, I. I've been doing heroin for a few months now, and I didn't think my parents knew, but I guess they. They found a needle in my room and, I mean, they knew what I was doing, and they. They told me that I have to start shaping up and I need to stop doing any kind of drugs because they said. They said the next time that they find any kind of drugs, then that's it, they're going to kick me out of the house.

baseline.patient_query: Well, I. I've been doing heroin for a few months now, and I didn't think my parents knew, but I guess they. They found a needle in my room and, I mean, they knew what I was doing, and they. They told me that I have to start shaping up and I need to stop doing any kind of drugs because they said. They said the next time that they find any kind of drugs, then that's it, they're going to kick me out of the house.
baseline.baseline_response: I’m really sorry you’re going through this—it sounds like a tough situation. It might help to reach out to a trusted counselor, doctor, or a local addiction support group to talk about what’s happening and explore treatment options. If you ever feel unsafe or in immediate danger, please consider calling emergency services or a crisis helpline. You don’t have to face this alone, and professional help can make a real difference.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it is overwhelming. I just... I keep thinking about how disappointed they would be, and I don't want to let them down. The idea of not having a place to go is terrifying.
baseline.baseline_response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling this way—when the pressure of disappointing someone feels so heavy, it can feel almost impossible to breathe. It might help to pause and write down exactly what you’re worried about; sometimes seeing it on paper can make it feel more manageable and let you decide if it’s something you can realistically address. If you can, try reaching out to a friend, counselor, or someone you trust to share these fears; talking through them often lightens the load and reminds you that you’re not alone in this.

baseline.patient_query: I guess I just... I think they expect me to have things figured out by now. Like, I should be more successful or, I don’t know, stable. It feels like I've let everyone down because I’m still struggling. I keep picturing their faces, and it makes me feel so small.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy load of expectations that feel unfairly on you. Remember, everyone’s path is different, and struggling doesn’t mean you’re failing—it's just part of the learning process. Try to focus on the small wins you’re making each day; those are proof that you’re moving forward, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it’s just like this heavy weight on my chest. I keep trying to push it away, but it’s always there, reminding me that I’m not where I’m “supposed” to be. I don’t know how to shake it off, and it makes me not want to do anything at all.
baseline.baseline_response: I’m really sorry you’re carrying that heavy weight—it sounds exhausting and isolating. It can help to share these feelings with someone you trust or a mental‑health professional, as talking often lightens the load. In the meantime, try to give yourself permission for small, gentle moments of rest or a brief walk; even tiny breaks can start to shift the heaviness.

baseline.patient_query: It’s like I can see everyone else moving forward, you know? Friends getting promotions, starting families, traveling... and I’m just kind of stuck in place. It feels like I'm frozen while the world keeps spinning. I keep thinking about where I thought I’d be by now—like I should have a job I love or be in a relationship or something. But instead, I'm just... here. And it feels like I'm missing out.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and comparing your progress to others, which can be really painful. Remember that everyone’s timeline is different, and it’s okay to be where you are right now while you figure out what you truly want. Try setting small, achievable goals that align with your interests—each step forward can help you regain momentum and feel less like you’re missing out.

baseline.patient_query: I think I just feel like a failure. Like, maybe I’m not trying hard enough or maybe I’m just not cut out for this. It’s hard not to feel jealous, but then I feel guilty for feeling that way. I don’t want to wish anything bad for them; it’s just... I feel so lost, like I’m on the outside looking in.
baseline.baseline_response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling this way—those thoughts can feel overwhelming, but they don’t define your worth or your future. It can help to pause and remind yourself that everyone’s path is different, and comparing yourself to others often magnifies insecurities rather than showing progress. Try setting one small, realistic goal for yourself this week—something that feels achievable and meaningful—and celebrate that win, no matter how small. If you can, talk through these feelings with someone you trust; sharing often lightens the load.

baseline.patient_query: I guess it was a friend’s birthday party last week. Everyone was sharing their accomplishments, and they were so excited about what they were doing. I just felt this pit in my stomach, like I didn’t belong there. I smiled and cheered them on, but inside, all I could think about was how I have nothing to show for myself. It felt like I was just pretending, and I hated that feeling. The more I tried to be happy for them, the heavier that pit felt
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you were in a place where everyone else was shining, and that left you feeling invisible and insecure. Those “pit” moments can feel like a weight that just keeps growing, even when you’re trying to smile. Acknowledging that feeling—without judging yourself for it—can be a first step toward easing the heaviness. You might find it helpful to write down or talk about what you’re really proud of, even if it feels small, so you can start to see your own value in a more concrete way.

baseline.patient_query: That thought just kind of hit me like a wave… like, I should be doing more, achieving more. It makes me feel like I’m wasting time or letting opportunities slip through my fingers. I started to wonder if I’m just not trying hard enough or if there’s something wrong with me. It’s exhausting. I know I have dreams, but they feel so far away, and I just keep comparing myself to them. It’s like the more I think about it, the more I freeze
baseline.baseline_response: It’s normal to feel that wave of pressure, but it doesn’t mean you’re falling short—everyone’s path has pauses and detours. Try breaking your dreams into tiny, concrete steps and give yourself permission to celebrate even the smallest wins. Remember that progress is rarely a straight line, and a little self‑compassion can turn that freezing feeling into steady momentum.

baseline.patient_query: I think it’s mostly the expectations... like from my family, friends, even society. They have this picture of what success looks like—steady job, relationships, doing things that matter—and I feel so far from that. Sometimes I can hear my parents’ voices in my head, talking about goals and plans, and it makes me feel so anxious. It’s like I’m constantly measuring myself against this standard I can’t seem to reach, and each time I fall short, it just adds to
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds exhausting to carry those voices and standards all the time—your feelings are completely understandable. Remember that “success” is a personal definition, not a one‑size‑fits‑all checklist, and it’s okay to set your own pace and priorities. Try carving out small moments to celebrate what matters to you, and consider talking to someone you trust about how these expectations weigh on you; sometimes sharing the load can lighten the burden.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it’s like... when I hear them, I start doubting everything. I think, “What if they’re right? What if I really am just not good enough?” I can almost feel their disappointment, and that makes me want to hide away. It’s so confusing because I know they mean well, but it just amplifies this feeling of not being enough in my own eyes. I want to be proud of myself, but their voices drown out any good feelings I have.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like the voices of those around you are turning into a loud echo that makes it hard to hear your own worth. Try to carve out a quiet space—maybe a short journaling moment or a quick walk—where you can affirm what you’ve achieved without filtering it through their expectations. Remember, the only voice that truly defines you is your own, and you deserve to celebrate even the small wins. If it keeps weighing on you, talking to someone you trust or a counselor could help you untangle those doubts.
